1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the opening of the last chapter of the book — the law of vows, things freely promised to God. The witnesses note that Gill notes the body of laws was finished in the preceding chapter, and here are added rules concerning vows, which a man was not obliged to make but did of his own freewill. The witnesses note that the chapter treats of what is voluntarily dedicated to the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the after the laws — the body of commanded laws now finished; the vows — what a man freely promises to God; the of freewill — not obliged, but of his own good pleasure.

The witnesses agree the book closes with the freewill offering of the heart: after all the commanded laws, God gives rules for what a man might vow of his own accord; the law that bound is followed by the love that gives, and even the freewill gift is brought under order, that devotion be sincere and not rash.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of vows: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the after the laws: the body of commanded laws now finished. The witnesses note the vows: what a man freely promises to God. The witnesses note the of freewill: not obliged, but of his own good pleasure. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the after the laws: the body of commanded laws now finished. The expositors observe the vows: what a man freely promises to God. The expositors observe the of freewill: not obliged, but of his own good pleasure. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the after the laws, the vows, the of freewill, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the book closes with the law of freewill vows — the commanded law followed by the love that gives, even the freewill gift brought under order.
2:
"Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, When a man shall make a singular vow, the persons shall be for the LORD by thy estimation." The witnesses note the first kind of vow — the vowing of persons, set apart for the LORD according to a fixed valuation. The witnesses note that Gill notes this concerned Israel only, the Gentiles excluded. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read "make a special vow," the souls being to the LORD according to Moses' valuation. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the to Israel — the law concerning the children of Israel; the a singular vow — a special vow of a person to the LORD; the for the LORD — the person set apart to God; the by valuation — discharged by a fixed money price.

The witnesses agree the vow of a person did not make him a temple-slave but laid on him a money valuation: when a man devoted himself or another to God, the law fixed a price by which the obligation was discharged, turning the rash or impossible literal dedication into an orderly gift to the sanctuary — devotion expressed in what could actually be rendered.

So the witnesses make the verse the vow of persons: The witnesses note the to Israel: the law concerning the children of Israel. The witnesses note the a singular vow: a special vow of a person to the LORD. The witnesses note the for the LORD: the person set apart to God. The witnesses note the by valuation: discharged by a fixed money price. The expositors observe the to Israel: the law concerning the children of Israel. The expositors observe the a singular vow: a special vow of a person to the LORD. The expositors observe the for the LORD: the person set apart to God. The expositors observe the by valuation: discharged by a fixed money price. The believer is taught to mark the to Israel, the a singular vow, the for the LORD, the by valuation, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the vow of a person was discharged by a fixed valuation — the literal dedication turned into an orderly gift the sanctuary could receive.
3:
"And thy estimation shall be of the male from twenty years old even unto sixty years old... fifty shekels of silver, after the shekel of the sanctuary." The witnesses note the highest valuation — fifty shekels for a man in the prime of life, twenty to sixty years. The witnesses note that Gill notes the estimation was not left to the man's or priest's pleasure but fixed by these rules, according to age. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch give the table — fifty shekels for a man of twenty to sixty. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the male — the man in his prime, twenty to sixty; the fifty shekels — the highest fixed price; the the sanctuary shekel — reckoned by the standard weight; the a fixed scale — the price set by rule, not caprice.

The witnesses agree the valuations were set by a fixed scale, not by caprice: the man in his working prime was rated highest, the price reckoned after the standard shekel of the sanctuary; the law thus guarded the vow from both the rash overvaluing of zeal and the covetous undervaluing of greed, every dedication measured by a just and constant rule.

So the witnesses make the verse the highest valuation: The witnesses note the the male: the man in his prime, twenty to sixty. The witnesses note the fifty shekels: the highest fixed price. The witnesses note the the sanctuary shekel: reckoned by the standard weight. The witnesses note the a fixed scale: the price set by rule, not caprice. The expositors observe the the male: the man in his prime, twenty to sixty. The expositors observe the fifty shekels: the highest fixed price. The expositors observe the the sanctuary shekel: reckoned by the standard weight. The expositors observe the a fixed scale: the price set by rule, not caprice. The believer is taught to mark the the male, the fifty shekels, the the sanctuary shekel, the a fixed scale, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the man in his prime was valued at fifty shekels by a fixed scale — the vow guarded from both rash zeal and covetous greed by a just and constant rule.
4:
"And if it be a female, then thy estimation shall be thirty shekels." The witnesses note the valuation of a woman of the same age — thirty shekels, somewhat more than half the man's. The witnesses note that Clarke explains the lower figure by the relative value of labour, the woman being of less use than the man in the service of the sanctuary, not of less personal worth. The witnesses note that Ellicott likewise grounds it in the lesser value of her labour, the woman being "the weaker vessel." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thirty shekels — the price for a woman of full age; the more than half — somewhat above half the man's rate; the value of labour — grounded in service, not personal worth; the not of the soul — no judgment on her dignity before God.

The witnesses agree the difference was one of working value, not of personal dignity: the valuations measured what each could render in service, and so the woman, whose strength for the heavier temple labour was less, was rated lower — the rule a matter of practical estimation in that economy, not a judgment on the soul, which God values without respect of persons.

So the witnesses make the verse the woman's valuation: The witnesses note the thirty shekels: the price for a woman of full age. The witnesses note the more than half: somewhat above half the man's rate. The witnesses note the value of labour: grounded in service, not personal worth. The witnesses note the not of the soul: no judgment on her dignity before God. The expositors observe the thirty shekels: the price for a woman of full age. The expositors observe the more than half: somewhat above half the man's rate. The expositors observe the value of labour: grounded in service, not personal worth. The expositors observe the not of the soul: no judgment on her dignity before God. The believer is taught to mark the thirty shekels, the more than half, the value of labour, the not of the soul, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the woman was valued at thirty shekels — a measure of working value in that economy, not of personal worth, which God esteems without respect of persons.
5:
"And if it be from five years old even unto twenty years old, then thy estimation shall be of the male twenty shekels, and for the female ten shekels." The witnesses note the valuation of the young — twenty shekels for a youth, ten for a maiden, between five and twenty. The witnesses note that Gill notes it is not the child who vows, but a grown person who says, "the estimation of this little one be upon me." The witnesses note that Clarke gives the figures, the boy twenty shekels, the girl ten. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the five to twenty — the valuation of youths and maidens; the twenty and ten shekels — the male and female rates; the vowed by another — a grown person taking the estimation upon him; the a true gift — the young as truly dedicated as the grown.

The witnesses agree the descending scale by age followed the same principle of service-value: the youth, not yet at full strength, was rated below the man, and the maiden below the youth, the proportion held steady through every stage; yet each, however small the price, was a true gift to God, the dedication of the young as real as that of the grown.

So the witnesses make the verse the young valued: The witnesses note the five to twenty: the valuation of youths and maidens. The witnesses note the twenty and ten shekels: the male and female rates. The witnesses note the vowed by another: a grown person taking the estimation upon him. The witnesses note the a true gift: the young as truly dedicated as the grown. The expositors observe the five to twenty: the valuation of youths and maidens. The expositors observe the twenty and ten shekels: the male and female rates. The expositors observe the vowed by another: a grown person taking the estimation upon him. The expositors observe the a true gift: the young as truly dedicated as the grown. The believer is taught to mark the five to twenty, the twenty and ten shekels, the vowed by another, the a true gift, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the youth was valued at twenty shekels and the maiden at ten — the scale of service-value held steady, yet each a true gift to God.
6:
"And if it be from a month old even unto five years old, then thy estimation shall be of the male five shekels... and for the female... three shekels of silver." The witnesses note the valuation of infants — five shekels for a male child, three for a female, from a month to five years. The witnesses note that Gill notes one under a month old might be vowed but not estimated, no valuation being made for it. The witnesses note that Clarke observes that, both being in infancy, the male and female were nearly of equal value, and that none were vowed under a month, the firstborn being already the LORD's. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a month to five years — the valuation of little children; the five and three shekels — the male and female rates, near equal; the none under a month — no estimation for the newborn; the the firstborn the LORD's — already his by another law.

The witnesses agree the law reached even to the cradle: the infant from a month old might be dedicated, though at the lowest price, the sexes near equal because neither yet laboured; below a month none was valued, since the firstborn already belonged to God by another law — the dedication of a child a tender act of faith, giving back to God the gift he had given.

So the witnesses make the verse the infant valued: The witnesses note the a month to five years: the valuation of little children. The witnesses note the five and three shekels: the male and female rates, near equal. The witnesses note the none under a month: no estimation for the newborn. The witnesses note the the firstborn the LORD's: already his by another law. The expositors observe the a month to five years: the valuation of little children. The expositors observe the five and three shekels: the male and female rates, near equal. The expositors observe the none under a month: no estimation for the newborn. The expositors observe the the firstborn the LORD's: already his by another law. The believer is taught to mark the a month to five years, the five and three shekels, the none under a month, the the firstborn the LORD's, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the infant was valued at five or three shekels — the dedication of a child a tender act of faith, giving back to God the gift he had given.
7:
"And if it be from sixty years old and above; if it be a male, then thy estimation shall be fifteen shekels, and for the female ten shekels." The witnesses note the valuation of the aged — fifteen shekels for an old man, ten for an old woman, past their working years. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of one almost past his labour, when it is time to leave off business. The witnesses note that Clarke and Ellicott ground the lower figure, as with the children, in the diminished value of labour in age. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sixty and above — the valuation of the old; the fifteen and ten shekels — the male and female rates in age; the past labour — the strength for service spent; the age not despised — the aged still received as a gift to God.

The witnesses agree the scale closed where it had begun, with the lowest valuations: the aged, like the very young, were rated below the prime because their strength for service was spent, the man and woman near equal in their decline; yet age was not despised, for the old man might still be vowed and his dedication received — the gift of a life nearly run as welcome as that of one in its prime.

So the witnesses make the verse the aged valued: The witnesses note the sixty and above: the valuation of the old. The witnesses note the fifteen and ten shekels: the male and female rates in age. The witnesses note the past labour: the strength for service spent. The witnesses note the age not despised: the aged still received as a gift to God. The expositors observe the sixty and above: the valuation of the old. The expositors observe the fifteen and ten shekels: the male and female rates in age. The expositors observe the past labour: the strength for service spent. The expositors observe the age not despised: the aged still received as a gift to God. The believer is taught to mark the sixty and above, the fifteen and ten shekels, the past labour, the age not despised, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the aged were valued at fifteen or ten shekels — the scale closing low as it began, yet age not despised, the gift of a life nearly run still welcome.
8:
"But if he be poorer than thy estimation, then he shall present himself before the priest, and the priest shall value him; according to his ability... shall the priest value him." The witnesses note the merciful provision for the poor — he who could not pay the fixed price was valued by the priest according to his ability. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is for one so poor he is unable to pay the value set upon him. The witnesses note that Ellicott describes the man pleading poverty appearing before the priest, who examined his circumstances and taxed him accordingly. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the poorer than the estimation — unable to pay the fixed price; the before the priest — his circumstances examined; the according to his ability — valued at what he could pay; the the willing heart — the gift received by what he had.

The witnesses agree the law would not let poverty bar a man from giving: where the fixed valuation was beyond his means, the priest weighed his ability and set a price he could pay, that the poor man's vow be received as truly as the rich man's; God measured the gift by what a man had, not by a rigid sum — the willing heart accepted according to that it had (cf. 2 Cor. 8:12).

So the witnesses make the verse the poor man's vow: The witnesses note the poorer than the estimation: unable to pay the fixed price. The witnesses note the before the priest: his circumstances examined. The witnesses note the according to his ability: valued at what he could pay. The witnesses note the the willing heart: the gift received by what he had. The expositors observe the poorer than the estimation: unable to pay the fixed price. The expositors observe the before the priest: his circumstances examined. The expositors observe the according to his ability: valued at what he could pay. The expositors observe the the willing heart: the gift received by what he had. The believer is taught to mark the poorer than the estimation, the before the priest, the according to his ability, the the willing heart, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the poor man was valued by his ability — God receiving the gift by what a man had, the willing heart accepted according to that it had (2 Cor. 8:12).
9:
"And if it be a beast, whereof men bring an offering unto the LORD, all that any man giveth of such unto the LORD shall be holy." The witnesses note the vow of a clean beast — a creature fit for sacrifice, once given to the LORD, was holy and irrevocably his. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of creatures used in sacrifice — bullocks, sheep, goats, rams, lambs. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read that everything so vowed was to be holy and not exchanged, good for bad or bad for good. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the fit for offering — a creature used in sacrifice; the given to the LORD — vowed and dedicated to him; the shall be holy — passing wholly into God's ownership; the not reclaimed — the gift the LORD's outright.

The witnesses agree that what was fit for the altar, once vowed, passed wholly into God's ownership: a clean beast dedicated could not be taken back or bartered, for it was now holy; the gift, once made, was the LORD's outright — a lesson that what is truly given to God is given for good, not held with a hand ready to reclaim it.

So the witnesses make the verse the vow of a clean beast: The witnesses note the fit for offering: a creature used in sacrifice. The witnesses note the given to the LORD: vowed and dedicated to him. The witnesses note the shall be holy: passing wholly into God's ownership. The witnesses note the not reclaimed: the gift the LORD's outright. The expositors observe the fit for offering: a creature used in sacrifice. The expositors observe the given to the LORD: vowed and dedicated to him. The expositors observe the shall be holy: passing wholly into God's ownership. The expositors observe the not reclaimed: the gift the LORD's outright. The believer is taught to mark the fit for offering, the given to the LORD, the shall be holy, the not reclaimed, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that a clean beast once vowed was holy and the LORD's outright — what is truly given to God given for good, not held with a hand ready to reclaim it.
10:
"He shall not alter it nor change it, a good for a bad, or a bad for a good: and if he shall at all change beast for beast, then it and the exchange thereof shall be holy." The witnesses note the prohibition of exchanging the vowed beast — no swapping good for bad or bad for good, and if any swapped, both became holy. The witnesses note that Gill, with Abarbinel, distinguishes "alter" (for one of another kind) from "change" (for one of the same kind). The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note that if he changed beast for beast, both the first and the exchange were holy. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not alter nor change — no swapping the vowed beast; the good for bad — no bettering one's bargain; the both holy — god claiming both if any change be made; the no evasion — what is vowed is vowed, craft only doubling it.

The witnesses agree the law shut the door on every evasion: a man might not better his bargain by substituting a worse beast, nor pretend to improve the gift by a better; and the penalty for attempting any exchange was that God claimed both animals — the rule sternly teaching that what is vowed is vowed, and that craft in dealing with God only doubles the obligation.

So the witnesses make the verse against exchange: The witnesses note the not alter nor change: no swapping the vowed beast. The witnesses note the good for bad: no bettering one's bargain. The witnesses note the both holy: God claiming both if any change be made. The witnesses note the no evasion: what is vowed is vowed, craft only doubling it. The expositors observe the not alter nor change: no swapping the vowed beast. The expositors observe the good for bad: no bettering one's bargain. The expositors observe the both holy: God claiming both if any change be made. The expositors observe the no evasion: what is vowed is vowed, craft only doubling it. The believer is taught to mark the not alter nor change, the good for bad, the both holy, the no evasion, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the vowed beast might not be exchanged, and any attempt made both holy — craft in dealing with God only doubling the obligation.
11:
"And if it be any unclean beast, of which they do not offer a sacrifice unto the LORD, then he shall present the beast before the priest:" The witnesses note the vow of an unclean beast — one unfit for sacrifice, to be presented to the priest for valuation. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of creatures not offered, besides the clean kinds, or even of blemished beasts unfit for the altar. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads it of an unclean beast not belonging to the three sacrificial kinds, which cannot be offered. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the an unclean beast — one not offered in sacrifice; the not for the altar — useless as a sacrifice itself; the before the priest — presented to be valued; the turned to account — its worth serving the sanctuary.

The witnesses agree the law made room for vowing what could not be sacrificed: an unclean or blemished animal, useless at the altar, was not refused but brought to the priest to be valued, that its worth in money might still serve the sanctuary; nothing a man devoted in sincerity was wasted, even the unfit turned to account for the house of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the unclean beast vowed: The witnesses note the an unclean beast: one not offered in sacrifice. The witnesses note the not for the altar: useless as a sacrifice itself. The witnesses note the before the priest: presented to be valued. The witnesses note the turned to account: its worth serving the sanctuary. The expositors observe the an unclean beast: one not offered in sacrifice. The expositors observe the not for the altar: useless as a sacrifice itself. The expositors observe the before the priest: presented to be valued. The expositors observe the turned to account: its worth serving the sanctuary. The believer is taught to mark the an unclean beast, the not for the altar, the before the priest, the turned to account, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that an unclean beast vowed was brought to the priest to be valued — nothing devoted in sincerity wasted, even the unfit turned to account for the house of God.
12:
"And the priest shall value it, whether it be good or bad: as thou valuest it, who art the priest, so shall it be." The witnesses note the priest's valuation of the unclean beast — his estimate, good or bad, standing as the price. The witnesses note that Gill notes the priest put a price upon it according to its worth as it appeared to him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch add that if the owner would redeem it, he was to add a fifth above the valuation (v.13). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the priest values it — setting the price by its worth; the good or bad — his estimate of its condition; the so shall it be — his appraisal made final; the order and honesty — dispute prevented by his fixed word.

The witnesses agree the priest's judgment was made final to prevent dispute: his appraisal of the beast's worth fixed the sum, neither owner nor onlooker overruling it; the appointed servant of God set the value, and the gift was reckoned by his word — a guard of order and honesty in every dealing with the sanctuary.

So the witnesses make the verse the priest's valuation: The witnesses note the the priest values it: setting the price by its worth. The witnesses note the good or bad: his estimate of its condition. The witnesses note the so shall it be: his appraisal made final. The witnesses note the order and honesty: dispute prevented by his fixed word. The expositors observe the the priest values it: setting the price by its worth. The expositors observe the good or bad: his estimate of its condition. The expositors observe the so shall it be: his appraisal made final. The expositors observe the order and honesty: dispute prevented by his fixed word. The believer is taught to mark the the priest values it, the good or bad, the so shall it be, the order and honesty, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the priest's valuation of the beast was made final — the appointed servant fixing the price, a guard of order and honesty in dealing with the sanctuary.
13:
"But if he will at all redeem it, then he shall add a fifth part thereof unto thy estimation." The witnesses note the law of redeeming a vowed thing — a fifth part added to the priest's valuation. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is for the owner determined to have his beast again at any rate. The witnesses note that Clarke sees the added fifth as a check on rash vows and covetous redemptions, the priest alone valuing the thing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the if he redeem it — the owner buying his vowed thing back; the add a fifth — a surcharge above the valuation; the against rash vows — discouraging hasty vowing and grasping back; the just compensation — the sanctuary recompensed for the thing reclaimed.

The witnesses agree the surcharge served a double end: it discouraged the man from vowing rashly and then grasping his gift back, and it gave the sanctuary a just compensation for the thing reclaimed; the fifth added made redemption cost more than the bare worth, so that a vow once made could not be lightly undone — devotion was not to be a thing of second thoughts.

So the witnesses make the verse the added fifth: The witnesses note the if he redeem it: the owner buying his vowed thing back. The witnesses note the add a fifth: a surcharge above the valuation. The witnesses note the against rash vows: discouraging hasty vowing and grasping back. The witnesses note the just compensation: the sanctuary recompensed for the thing reclaimed. The expositors observe the if he redeem it: the owner buying his vowed thing back. The expositors observe the add a fifth: a surcharge above the valuation. The expositors observe the against rash vows: discouraging hasty vowing and grasping back. The expositors observe the just compensation: the sanctuary recompensed for the thing reclaimed. The believer is taught to mark the if he redeem it, the add a fifth, the against rash vows, the just compensation, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that redemption cost a fifth above the valuation — a check on rash vows and covetous reclaiming, devotion not to be a thing of second thoughts.
14:
"And when a man shall sanctify his house to be holy unto the LORD, then the priest shall estimate it, whether it be good or bad: as the priest shall estimate it, so shall it stand." The witnesses note the vow of a house — set apart to the LORD, valued by the priest, the estimate standing as the price. The witnesses note that Gill notes the house was devoted to holy uses, that it might be sold and the money laid out on sacrifices or the repairs of the temple. The witnesses note that other goods are comprehended under this, the Jewish writers adding rules for debts and a wife's dowry. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sanctify his house — dedicating his dwelling to the LORD; the the priest estimates it — valued good or bad by the priest; the for holy uses — sold, the money serving the sanctuary; the the whole estate — house and goods open to devotion.

The witnesses agree a man might dedicate his very dwelling to God: the house given was valued by the priest and its worth turned to the service of the sanctuary; the law shows that no part of a man's estate lay outside the reach of devotion — house and goods, not persons and cattle only, might be laid upon God's altar, the whole of life open to be given.

So the witnesses make the verse the vow of a house: The witnesses note the sanctify his house: dedicating his dwelling to the LORD. The witnesses note the the priest estimates it: valued good or bad by the priest. The witnesses note the for holy uses: sold, the money serving the sanctuary. The witnesses note the the whole estate: house and goods open to devotion. The expositors observe the sanctify his house: dedicating his dwelling to the LORD. The expositors observe the the priest estimates it: valued good or bad by the priest. The expositors observe the for holy uses: sold, the money serving the sanctuary. The expositors observe the the whole estate: house and goods open to devotion. The believer is taught to mark the sanctify his house, the the priest estimates it, the for holy uses, the the whole estate, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that a man might dedicate his house to God, valued by the priest — no part of a man's estate outside the reach of devotion, the whole of life open to be given.
15:
"And if he that sanctified it will redeem his house, then he shall add the fifth part of the money of thy estimation unto it, and it shall be his." The witnesses note that a vowed house might be redeemed by its owner — adding a fifth to the valuation to recover it. The witnesses note that Gill notes a house set apart might be redeemed, by another paying the price or by the original owner adding a fifth. The witnesses note that, citing Maimonides, the house, whether in a walled city or a village, might always be redeemed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he that sanctified it — the owner who vowed the house; the add a fifth — the surcharge to recover it; the always redeemable — whether in walled city or village; the the balance — freedom to redeem with a charge honouring the vow.

The witnesses agree the same gracious rule of redemption governed the house as the beast: the owner who repented of his gift might have it again, but only at a fifth above the price, so that his recovery cost him something and the sanctuary lost nothing; the law balanced the freedom to redeem with a charge that honoured the original devotion.

So the witnesses make the verse redeeming the house: The witnesses note the he that sanctified it: the owner who vowed the house. The witnesses note the add a fifth: the surcharge to recover it. The witnesses note the always redeemable: whether in walled city or village. The witnesses note the the balance: freedom to redeem with a charge honouring the vow. The expositors observe the he that sanctified it: the owner who vowed the house. The expositors observe the add a fifth: the surcharge to recover it. The expositors observe the always redeemable: whether in walled city or village. The expositors observe the the balance: freedom to redeem with a charge honouring the vow. The believer is taught to mark the he that sanctified it, the add a fifth, the always redeemable, the the balance, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the vowed house might be redeemed at a fifth above its price — the freedom to recover balanced with a charge that honoured the original devotion.
16:
"And if a man shall sanctify unto the LORD some part of a field of his possession, then thy estimation shall be according to the seed thereof..." The witnesses note the vow of a field of inheritance — valued by the seed it required, that is, by its size and yield. The witnesses note that Gill notes the field of possession was the inheritance from his father, distinguished from a field of purchase, and that only part might be devoted, that the family keep a livelihood and the estate not pass from the tribe. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the valuation by the field of his hereditary property. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a field of possession — the hereditary inheritance from his father; the by the seed thereof — valued by its size and yield; the only part — that the family keep a livelihood; the the inheritance guarded — the tribal land not alienated for good.

The witnesses agree the law guarded the family inheritance even in devotion: a man might dedicate part of his patrimony but not so as to strip his household or alienate the tribal land for good; the field was valued by the seed it took, a fair measure of its worth, and the gift bounded by the larger law that kept Israel's land within its families.

So the witnesses make the verse the vow of a field: The witnesses note the a field of possession: the hereditary inheritance from his father. The witnesses note the by the seed thereof: valued by its size and yield. The witnesses note the only part: that the family keep a livelihood. The witnesses note the the inheritance guarded: the tribal land not alienated for good. The expositors observe the a field of possession: the hereditary inheritance from his father. The expositors observe the by the seed thereof: valued by its size and yield. The expositors observe the only part: that the family keep a livelihood. The expositors observe the the inheritance guarded: the tribal land not alienated for good. The believer is taught to mark the a field of possession, the by the seed thereof, the only part, the the inheritance guarded, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that a man might vow part of his hereditary field, valued by its seed — devotion bounded by the law that kept Israel's land within its families.
17:
"If he sanctify his field from the year of jubile, according to thy estimation it shall stand." The witnesses note the field vowed at the jubilee — its full valuation standing, since the whole span to the next jubilee remained. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of one who sanctifies the field at or just after the jubilee year, so that a purchaser comes at once. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read that if he sanctified it immediately after the jubilee, no alteration was made in the valuation. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from the year of jubile — vowed at or just after that year; the the full estimation — the whole span of years reckoned in; the it shall stand — no alteration in the valuation; the tied to the jubilee — the price set by the years of use.

The witnesses agree the timing of the vow set its price: a field given just after the jubilee carried its full estimated worth, for the whole stretch of years to the next jubilee was reckoned in it; the valuation was tied to the time the sanctuary would have the use of the land, the jubilee law of the land threading through even the law of vows.

So the witnesses make the verse the field vowed at jubilee: The witnesses note the from the year of jubile: vowed at or just after that year. The witnesses note the the full estimation: the whole span of years reckoned in. The witnesses note the it shall stand: no alteration in the valuation. The witnesses note the tied to the jubilee: the price set by the years of use. The expositors observe the from the year of jubile: vowed at or just after that year. The expositors observe the the full estimation: the whole span of years reckoned in. The expositors observe the it shall stand: no alteration in the valuation. The expositors observe the tied to the jubilee: the price set by the years of use. The believer is taught to mark the from the year of jubile, the the full estimation, the it shall stand, the tied to the jubilee, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that a field vowed at the jubilee stood at its full valuation — the price tied to the years of use, the jubilee law of the land threading through the law of vows.
18:
"But if he sanctify his field after the jubile, then the priest shall reckon unto him the money according to the years that remain... and it shall be abated from thy estimation." The witnesses note the field vowed later — its price abated in proportion to the years already passed since the jubilee. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of one who sanctifies the field some years after the jubilee, halfway or nearer to the next. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the priest reckoning the money by the years remaining to the jubilee, the price abated accordingly. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the after the jubilee — vowed some years into the cycle; the the years that remain — reckoned to the next jubilee; the abated — the price lessened as the years pass; the exact and fair — valued by what it yields before the return.

The witnesses agree the valuation fell as the jubilee drew nearer: since the sanctuary would have the field only until the next jubilee returned it, the fewer the years left, the less the price; the reckoning was exact and fair, the gift valued by what it would actually yield to God before the land's appointed return.

So the witnesses make the verse the field vowed later: The witnesses note the after the jubilee: vowed some years into the cycle. The witnesses note the the years that remain: reckoned to the next jubilee. The witnesses note the abated: the price lessened as the years pass. The witnesses note the exact and fair: valued by what it yields before the return. The expositors observe the after the jubilee: vowed some years into the cycle. The expositors observe the the years that remain: reckoned to the next jubilee. The expositors observe the abated: the price lessened as the years pass. The expositors observe the exact and fair: valued by what it yields before the return. The believer is taught to mark the after the jubilee, the the years that remain, the abated, the exact and fair, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that a field vowed later was valued at an abated price, by the years remaining — the gift reckoned by what it would yield to God before the land's appointed return.
19:
"And if he that sanctified the field will in any wise redeem it, then he shall add the fifth part of the money of thy estimation unto it, and it shall be assured to him." The witnesses note the owner redeeming his vowed field — adding a fifth, and the field made sure to him again. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of one desirous and determined to have his field again, repenting that he parted with it. The witnesses note that Ellicott observes the low value per acre was set precisely that the field might be redeemed by him, his wife and heirs sharing the right. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the will redeem it — the owner desiring his field again; the add the fifth — the surcharge to recover it; the assured to him — the patrimony made sure again; the toward keeping it — the low value inviting redemption.

The witnesses agree the law leaned toward keeping the field in the family: the valuation was set low enough to invite redemption, and the owner who added the fifth had his patrimony assured to him again; the whole structure encouraged the Israelite to recover his inheritance, that the land devoted in a moment's zeal not be lost from his house for good.

So the witnesses make the verse redeeming the field: The witnesses note the will redeem it: the owner desiring his field again. The witnesses note the add the fifth: the surcharge to recover it. The witnesses note the assured to him: the patrimony made sure again. The witnesses note the toward keeping it: the low value inviting redemption. The expositors observe the will redeem it: the owner desiring his field again. The expositors observe the add the fifth: the surcharge to recover it. The expositors observe the assured to him: the patrimony made sure again. The expositors observe the toward keeping it: the low value inviting redemption. The believer is taught to mark the will redeem it, the add the fifth, the assured to him, the toward keeping it, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the owner might redeem his vowed field at a fifth above the price — the law leaning to keep the inheritance in the family, the patrimony assured to him again.
20:
"And if he will not redeem the field, or if he have sold the field to another man, it shall not be redeemed any more." The witnesses note the case of the man who would not redeem his field, or had sold it on — losing the right of redemption. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of one who will not give the fifth besides, but lets it be disposed of for the holy uses he devoted it to. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads it of one base enough to forego the privilege of redemption, showing no desire to perpetuate his family name. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the will not redeem — the owner refusing to recover it; the or sold to another — having bargained the field away; the not redeemed any more — the right of recovery lost; the his own neglect — the loss falling on his carelessness or greed.

The witnesses agree the forfeiture fell on the man's own neglect: every door to recover his land had been opened, and if he still would not redeem it, or had bargained it away to another, he lost it for good; the law had done all to keep the inheritance with him, and only his own carelessness or greed could finally part him from it.

So the witnesses make the verse the forfeited field: The witnesses note the will not redeem: the owner refusing to recover it. The witnesses note the or sold to another: having bargained the field away. The witnesses note the not redeemed any more: the right of recovery lost. The witnesses note the his own neglect: the loss falling on his carelessness or greed. The expositors observe the will not redeem: the owner refusing to recover it. The expositors observe the or sold to another: having bargained the field away. The expositors observe the not redeemed any more: the right of recovery lost. The expositors observe the his own neglect: the loss falling on his carelessness or greed. The believer is taught to mark the will not redeem, the or sold to another, the not redeemed any more, the his own neglect, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the man who would not redeem his field, or had sold it on, lost it for good — the loss falling on his own neglect, the law having done all to keep it with him.
21:
"But the field, when it goeth out in the jubile, shall be holy unto the LORD, as a field devoted; the possession thereof shall be the priest's." The witnesses note the end of the unredeemed field — at the jubilee it became holy to the LORD as a devoted field, the priest's possession. The witnesses note that Gill notes the field then went out of the hand of the buyer. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read it becoming holy to the LORD as a devoted field, falling to the priest. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the out in the jubilee — the field released at the jubilee; the holy unto the LORD — become a devoted field to God; the the priest's — falling to God's ministers as his portion; the the vow made good — the neglected gift fulfilled in the end.

The witnesses agree the unredeemed field did not return to its owner but passed permanently to the LORD's ministers: what a man had devoted and then neglected to redeem, the jubilee did not restore to him, but gave to the priests as God's portion; the carelessly held vow was made good in the end, the land lost to the man becoming holy to the God he had vowed it to.

So the witnesses make the verse the devoted field: The witnesses note the out in the jubilee: the field released at the jubilee. The witnesses note the holy unto the LORD: become a devoted field to God. The witnesses note the the priest's: falling to God's ministers as his portion. The witnesses note the the vow made good: the neglected gift fulfilled in the end. The expositors observe the out in the jubilee: the field released at the jubilee. The expositors observe the holy unto the LORD: become a devoted field to God. The expositors observe the the priest's: falling to God's ministers as his portion. The expositors observe the the vow made good: the neglected gift fulfilled in the end. The believer is taught to mark the out in the jubilee, the holy unto the LORD, the the priest's, the the vow made good, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the unredeemed field passed at the jubilee to the priests as God's portion — the carelessly held vow made good in the end, the land becoming holy to the God he had vowed it to.
22:
"And if a man sanctify unto the LORD a field which he hath bought, which is not of the fields of his possession;" The witnesses note the vow of a purchased field — one bought, not inherited, held only until the next jubilee. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is a field bought with his own money from one in distress, which by the former law returned to the original owner at the jubilee. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads it of a field acquired by purchase, his only till the next jubilee, when it reverts to its first owner. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a field he hath bought — acquired by purchase, not inheritance; the not of his possession — not his patrimony; the held till the jubilee — a lease reverting at the jubilee; the vow within his right — he could vow no more than he owned.

The witnesses agree the purchased field was a different case because the man held only a lease of it: he could vow no more than he owned, and what he owned was the use of the land until the jubilee; so the law that follows deals with such a leased field accordingly, careful that a man's devotion not reach beyond his right or wrong the field's true owner.

So the witnesses make the verse the purchased field: The witnesses note the a field he hath bought: acquired by purchase, not inheritance. The witnesses note the not of his possession: not his patrimony. The witnesses note the held till the jubilee: a lease reverting at the jubilee. The witnesses note the vow within his right: he could vow no more than he owned. The expositors observe the a field he hath bought: acquired by purchase, not inheritance. The expositors observe the not of his possession: not his patrimony. The expositors observe the held till the jubilee: a lease reverting at the jubilee. The expositors observe the vow within his right: he could vow no more than he owned. The believer is taught to mark the a field he hath bought, the not of his possession, the held till the jubilee, the vow within his right, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the vow of a purchased field was a different case, the man holding only a lease till the jubilee — devotion not reaching beyond his right nor wronging the true owner.
23:
"Then the priest shall reckon unto him the worth of thy estimation, even unto the year of the jubile: and he shall give thine estimation in that day, as a holy thing unto the LORD." The witnesses note the valuation of the purchased field — reckoned to the jubilee, the sum paid at once as a holy thing. The witnesses note that Gill notes the priest estimated the field and set a price by the years remaining to the jubilee. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read him giving the estimation that day as a holy thing unto the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the reckon to the jubilee — the price by the years he holds it; the given that day — the sum paid at once; the a holy thing — dedicated to the LORD; the bounded gift — the worth of what was his to give, no more.

The witnesses agree the leased field was valued strictly for the term the man held it: since his right ran only to the jubilee, the price covered only those years, and was paid at once into the sanctuary as holy; the gift was real but bounded, the man giving God the full worth of what was truly his to give and no more.

So the witnesses make the verse the leased field valued: The witnesses note the reckon to the jubilee: the price by the years he holds it. The witnesses note the given that day: the sum paid at once. The witnesses note the a holy thing: dedicated to the LORD. The witnesses note the bounded gift: the worth of what was his to give, no more. The expositors observe the reckon to the jubilee: the price by the years he holds it. The expositors observe the given that day: the sum paid at once. The expositors observe the a holy thing: dedicated to the LORD. The expositors observe the bounded gift: the worth of what was his to give, no more. The believer is taught to mark the reckon to the jubilee, the given that day, the a holy thing, the bounded gift, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the purchased field was valued only to the jubilee and paid at once as holy — the man giving God the full worth of what was truly his, and no more.
24:
"In the year of the jubile the field shall return unto him of whom it was bought, even to him to whom the possession of the land did belong." The witnesses note the purchased field returning at the jubilee — not to the vower nor the buyer, but to its hereditary owner. The witnesses note that Gill notes it returned not to him that sanctified it, nor to him that bought it of the temple treasurer, but to the original proprietor of whom it was first bought. The witnesses note that Ellicott grounds this in the law of Lev. 25:23-28, the land returning to its hereditary owner. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the in the jubilee — at the appointed year of release; the not to vower or buyer — passing neither to them; the to the hereditary owner — back to whom the land belonged; the the LORD's land — the divine grant overruling all.

The witnesses agree the jubilee law of the land overruled all: however a field passed through vow and valuation, at the jubilee it came back to the family to whom God had given it, for the land was the LORD's and the tribes were but its stewards; even devotion could not break the divine grant that kept each inheritance in its house forever.

So the witnesses make the verse the field's return: The witnesses note the in the jubilee: at the appointed year of release. The witnesses note the not to vower or buyer: passing neither to them. The witnesses note the to the hereditary owner: back to whom the land belonged. The witnesses note the the LORD's land: the divine grant overruling all. The expositors observe the in the jubilee: at the appointed year of release. The expositors observe the not to vower or buyer: passing neither to them. The expositors observe the to the hereditary owner: back to whom the land belonged. The expositors observe the the LORD's land: the divine grant overruling all. The believer is taught to mark the in the jubilee, the not to vower or buyer, the to the hereditary owner, the the LORD's land, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the purchased field returned at the jubilee to its hereditary owner — the LORD's land kept in its family, even devotion not breaking the divine grant.
25:
"And all thy estimation shall be according to the shekel of the sanctuary: twenty gerahs shall be the shekel." The witnesses note that every valuation was reckoned by the shekel of the sanctuary — the fixed standard weight. The witnesses note that Gill notes the sanctuary shekel was the standard of all shekels, of full weight and worth, and the payment made in such. The witnesses note that Clarke calls it the standard shekel, kept in the sanctuary to try and regulate all the weights in the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the standard — the sanctuary shekel of full weight; the twenty gerahs — the fixed measure of the shekel; the all valuations by it — every vow reckoned by the one weight; the honesty guarded — no cheating by a light shekel.

The witnesses agree the single standard guarded honesty in every vow: all valuations were measured by one fixed weight kept at the sanctuary, so that none could cheat by a light shekel or dispute the reckoning; the worship of God was bound to true measures, the same uprightness required in the holy place as in the market, the LORD's standard the rule of all.

So the witnesses make the verse the sanctuary shekel: The witnesses note the the standard: the sanctuary shekel of full weight. The witnesses note the twenty gerahs: the fixed measure of the shekel. The witnesses note the all valuations by it: every vow reckoned by the one weight. The witnesses note the honesty guarded: no cheating by a light shekel. The expositors observe the the standard: the sanctuary shekel of full weight. The expositors observe the twenty gerahs: the fixed measure of the shekel. The expositors observe the all valuations by it: every vow reckoned by the one weight. The expositors observe the honesty guarded: no cheating by a light shekel. The believer is taught to mark the the standard, the twenty gerahs, the all valuations by it, the honesty guarded, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that every valuation was reckoned by the sanctuary's standard shekel — the worship of God bound to true measures, his standard the rule of all.
26:
"Only the firstling of the beasts, which should be the LORD's firstling, no man shall sanctify it; whether it be ox, or sheep: it is the LORD's." The witnesses note that the firstborn of clean beasts could not be vowed — it was already the LORD's by law. The witnesses note that Gill notes the firstlings are excepted, for the reason that follows — they are already the LORD's. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch mark that what belonged to the LORD by law could not be dedicated to him by a vow (cf. Exod. 13:2). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the firstling — the firstborn of the clean beasts; the the LORD's already — his by the law of the firstling; the no man shall sanctify it — not a man's to vow; the the prior claim — the first and best already God's.

The witnesses agree a man could not give to God what was already his: the firstborn of the clean herd belonged to the LORD by the law of the firstling, and so was not a man's to vow; the rule teaches that true devotion offers what is one's own to give, and that the first and best already belongs to God by his prior claim — a claim fulfilled in Christ the firstborn, wholly the Father's.

So the witnesses make the verse the firstling reserved: The witnesses note the the firstling: the firstborn of the clean beasts. The witnesses note the the LORD's already: his by the law of the firstling. The witnesses note the no man shall sanctify it: not a man's to vow. The witnesses note the the prior claim: the first and best already God's. The expositors observe the the firstling: the firstborn of the clean beasts. The expositors observe the the LORD's already: his by the law of the firstling. The expositors observe the no man shall sanctify it: not a man's to vow. The expositors observe the the prior claim: the first and best already God's. The believer is taught to mark the the firstling, the the LORD's already, the no man shall sanctify it, the the prior claim, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the firstling could not be vowed, being already the LORD's — true devotion offering what is one's own, the first and best already God's by his prior claim.
27:
"And if it be of an unclean beast, then he shall redeem it according to thine estimation, and shall add a fifth part of it thereto: or if it be not redeemed, then it shall be sold according to thy estimation." The witnesses note the firstling of an unclean beast — redeemed at the valuation with a fifth added, or else sold. The witnesses note that Gill distinguishes this from the firstling of unclean creatures redeemed with a lamb, reading it of such as were vowed for the repair of the sanctuary. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the unclean beast redeemed by the valuation with a fifth, or if not, sold by the valuation. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the an unclean beast — the firstling unfit for the altar; the redeem with a fifth — bought back at the valuation plus a fifth; the or sold — disposed of by the valuation if not redeemed; the turned to service — the unfit brought into the LORD's account.

The witnesses agree the unclean firstling, useless for the altar, was still turned to God's service by money: it could not be offered, so it was either redeemed at a price or sold and the proceeds given to the sanctuary; nothing devoted was wasted, the law finding a way to bring even the unfit firstborn into the LORD's account.

So the witnesses make the verse the unclean firstling: The witnesses note the an unclean beast: the firstling unfit for the altar. The witnesses note the redeem with a fifth: bought back at the valuation plus a fifth. The witnesses note the or sold: disposed of by the valuation if not redeemed. The witnesses note the turned to service: the unfit brought into the LORD's account. The expositors observe the an unclean beast: the firstling unfit for the altar. The expositors observe the redeem with a fifth: bought back at the valuation plus a fifth. The expositors observe the or sold: disposed of by the valuation if not redeemed. The expositors observe the turned to service: the unfit brought into the LORD's account. The believer is taught to mark the an unclean beast, the redeem with a fifth, the or sold, the turned to service, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the unclean firstling was redeemed with a fifth or sold — nothing devoted wasted, even the unfit firstborn brought into the LORD's account.
28:
"Notwithstanding no devoted thing, that a man shall devote unto the LORD of all that he hath... shall be sold or redeemed: every devoted thing is most holy unto the LORD." The witnesses note the devoted thing — the cherem — most holy, neither to be sold nor redeemed. The witnesses note that Gill distinguishes "devoting" from "sanctifying": the devoted thing carried a curse or execration upon misuse, and so was absolute and irrevocable, unlike things merely sanctified, which might be redeemed. The witnesses note that Clarke notes no devoted thing might be sold or redeemed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the devoted thing — the cherem, given by a binding vow; the a curse on misuse — an execration upon any other use; the not sold or redeemed — absolute and irrevocable; the most holy — wholly and forever the LORD's.

The witnesses agree the cherem was the most solemn of all dedications: a thing devoted by this binding vow passed wholly and forever to God, with a curse on any who turned it to other use, and could never be bought back; it stands as the strongest figure of an unreserved, irrevocable giving to God — the gift from which there is no taking back, most holy unto the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the devoted thing: The witnesses note the the devoted thing: the cherem, given by a binding vow. The witnesses note the a curse on misuse: an execration upon any other use. The witnesses note the not sold or redeemed: absolute and irrevocable. The witnesses note the most holy: wholly and forever the LORD's. The expositors observe the the devoted thing: the cherem, given by a binding vow. The expositors observe the a curse on misuse: an execration upon any other use. The expositors observe the not sold or redeemed: absolute and irrevocable. The expositors observe the most holy: wholly and forever the LORD's. The believer is taught to mark the the devoted thing, the a curse on misuse, the not sold or redeemed, the most holy, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the devoted thing was most holy, never sold or redeemed — the strongest figure of an unreserved, irrevocable giving to God, the gift from which there is no taking back.
29:
"None devoted, which shall be devoted of men, shall be redeemed; but shall surely be put to death." The witnesses note the gravest case — those devoted of men, that is, persons given over to destruction, who could not be redeemed but must die. The witnesses note that Gill carefully reads this not of men devoted to the LORD (as v.28) but of such as were devoted to ruin and destruction, for whom there was no redemption. The witnesses note that he instances those devoted by God himself — idolaters, the seven nations of Canaan, and especially the Amalekites — who were not to be spared but put to death. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the devoted of men — persons under the ban of destruction; the not the dedicated — not those devoted to the LORD as servants; the not redeemed — no ransom out of the sentence; the the divine justice — the Canaanites and Amalek under God's judgment.

The witnesses agree the verse speaks not of human sacrifice nor of the dedicated servant, but of the ban of destruction laid by God on the incorrigibly wicked: those whom God had marked for judgment — the Canaanite peoples and Amalek — could not be ransomed out of the sentence; the law executes the divine justice, the devoted-to-destruction passing irrevocably under it.

So the witnesses make the verse the devoted to destruction: The witnesses note the devoted of men: persons under the ban of destruction. The witnesses note the not the dedicated: not those devoted to the LORD as servants. The witnesses note the not redeemed: no ransom out of the sentence. The witnesses note the the divine justice: the Canaanites and Amalek under God's judgment. The expositors observe the devoted of men: persons under the ban of destruction. The expositors observe the not the dedicated: not those devoted to the LORD as servants. The expositors observe the not redeemed: no ransom out of the sentence. The expositors observe the the divine justice: the Canaanites and Amalek under God's judgment. The believer is taught to mark the devoted of men, the not the dedicated, the not redeemed, the the divine justice, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that those devoted to destruction could not be redeemed but died — not human sacrifice nor the dedicated servant, but the ban of God's justice on the incorrigibly wicked.
30:
"And all the tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land, or of the fruit of the tree, is the LORD's: it is holy unto the LORD." The witnesses note the tithe of the land — the tenth of seed and fruit — belonging to the LORD and holy to him. The witnesses note that Gill notes there were various tithes — the first tithe to the Levites, the second eaten at Jerusalem, and the poor's tithe in the third and sixth years. The witnesses note that the tenth of the land's increase was the LORD's by his own claim, holy unto him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seed and fruit — the tenth of the land's increase; the is the LORD's — his by his own claim, not freewill; the holy unto the LORD — set apart before the rest is touched; the his ownership — the whole increase acknowledged as God's.

The witnesses agree the tithe was not a freewill gift like the vow but a fixed due: a tenth of all the land yielded belonged to God of right, set apart as holy before the owner touched the rest; the tithe taught Israel that the whole increase came from God and that the first portion was his — a standing acknowledgment of his ownership of the land and its fruit (cf. Mal. 3:10).

So the witnesses make the verse the tithe of the land: The witnesses note the seed and fruit: the tenth of the land's increase. The witnesses note the is the LORD's: his by his own claim, not freewill. The witnesses note the holy unto the LORD: set apart before the rest is touched. The witnesses note the his ownership: the whole increase acknowledged as God's. The expositors observe the seed and fruit: the tenth of the land's increase. The expositors observe the is the LORD's: his by his own claim, not freewill. The expositors observe the holy unto the LORD: set apart before the rest is touched. The expositors observe the his ownership: the whole increase acknowledged as God's. The believer is taught to mark the seed and fruit, the is the LORD's, the holy unto the LORD, the his ownership, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the tithe of the land was the LORD's by right and holy to him — a standing acknowledgment that the whole increase comes from God and the first portion is his (Mal. 3:10).
31:
"And if a man will at all redeem ought of his tithes, he shall add thereto the fifth part thereof." The witnesses note that a man might redeem his tithe — turning it to money — by adding a fifth part. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of redeeming his own tithes, not his neighbour's, with the fifth added. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the same rule of the added fifth on redeeming the tithe. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the redeem his tithes — keeping the produce, paying its value; the add a fifth — the surcharge on redemption; the his own only — not his neighbour's tithe; the honouring the holy — the added portion marking it as the LORD's.

The witnesses agree the same surcharge guarded the tithe as the vow: a man who wished to keep his produce and pay its value instead must add a fifth, so that converting the holy thing to common use cost him something; the tithe could be turned to money for convenience, but not cheaply, the added portion honouring the holiness of what was the LORD's.

So the witnesses make the verse redeeming the tithe: The witnesses note the redeem his tithes: keeping the produce, paying its value. The witnesses note the add a fifth: the surcharge on redemption. The witnesses note the his own only: not his neighbour's tithe. The witnesses note the honouring the holy: the added portion marking it as the LORD's. The expositors observe the redeem his tithes: keeping the produce, paying its value. The expositors observe the add a fifth: the surcharge on redemption. The expositors observe the his own only: not his neighbour's tithe. The expositors observe the honouring the holy: the added portion marking it as the LORD's. The believer is taught to mark the redeem his tithes, the add a fifth, the his own only, the honouring the holy, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that a man might redeem his tithe by adding a fifth — converting the holy thing to common use costing him something, the surcharge honouring what was the LORD's.
32:
"And concerning the tithe of the herd, or of the flock, even of whatsoever passeth under the rod, the tenth shall be holy unto the LORD." The witnesses note the tithe of the cattle — every tenth beast that passed under the rod was holy to the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes this concerns oxen and sheep, the clean beasts only being tithed. The witnesses note that Clarke describes the rabbinic picture — the flock penned with a narrow gate, the owner standing with a rod dipped in dye, every tenth that passed marked as the LORD's. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the herd and flock — the clean cattle, oxen and sheep; the under the rod — the beasts counted one by one; the the tenth — every tenth marked as it passed; the holy unto the LORD — god's portion by a fair reckoning.

The witnesses agree the vivid image taught the impartiality of the tithe: the beasts were counted as they passed one by one under the herdsman's rod, and every tenth, whatever it chanced to be, was set apart as holy; the owner could not choose which to give, the lot of the count deciding, so that God received his portion by a fair and unmanipulated reckoning.

So the witnesses make the verse the tithe of the herd: The witnesses note the herd and flock: the clean cattle, oxen and sheep. The witnesses note the under the rod: the beasts counted one by one. The witnesses note the the tenth: every tenth marked as it passed. The witnesses note the holy unto the LORD: God's portion by a fair reckoning. The expositors observe the herd and flock: the clean cattle, oxen and sheep. The expositors observe the under the rod: the beasts counted one by one. The expositors observe the the tenth: every tenth marked as it passed. The expositors observe the holy unto the LORD: God's portion by a fair reckoning. The believer is taught to mark the herd and flock, the under the rod, the the tenth, the holy unto the LORD, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that every tenth beast passing under the rod was holy to the LORD — God receiving his portion by a fair and unmanipulated reckoning, the owner not choosing which to give.
33:
"He shall not search whether it be good or bad, neither shall he change it: and if he change it at all, then both it and the change thereof shall be holy; it shall not be redeemed." The witnesses note the rule against picking and swapping the tithed beast — the tenth taken as it came, and any exchange making both holy. The witnesses note that Gill notes the owner was not to search whether it be good or bad, fat or lean, perfect or blemished, but take it as it was. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read that if he changed it, both it and the change were holy and not redeemable; Ellicott adds he was not to pick out the good ones for himself. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not search good or bad — the tenth taken as it came; the not change it — no swapping the marked beast; the both holy — god claiming both if any change be made; the rendered honestly — the tithe given without human cheating.

The witnesses agree the law forbade all craft in the tithing: the owner might not sort the flock to give God the worst, nor swap a marked beast for another, but took whatever the rod fell on; and any attempt to exchange was punished by God claiming both — the tithe rendered honestly, the LORD given his tenth without the cheat of human choosing.

So the witnesses make the verse against picking and swapping: The witnesses note the not search good or bad: the tenth taken as it came. The witnesses note the not change it: no swapping the marked beast. The witnesses note the both holy: God claiming both if any change be made. The witnesses note the rendered honestly: the tithe given without human cheating. The expositors observe the not search good or bad: the tenth taken as it came. The expositors observe the not change it: no swapping the marked beast. The expositors observe the both holy: God claiming both if any change be made. The expositors observe the rendered honestly: the tithe given without human cheating. The believer is taught to mark the not search good or bad, the not change it, the both holy, the rendered honestly, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the tithed beast was taken as it came and never swapped, any exchange making both holy — the tithe rendered honestly, without the cheat of human choosing.
34:
"These are the commandments, which the LORD commanded Moses for the children of Israel in mount Sinai." The witnesses note the closing colophon of the book — all these commandments given to Moses for Israel at Sinai. The witnesses note that Gill takes it of the commandments of this chapter, or rather of the whole book, delivered to Moses for the children of Israel, priests and people. The witnesses note that they were given at Mount Sinai, after the tabernacle was set up and the LORD spake out of it (Lev. 1:1). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the these are the commandments — the laws of the book gathered up; the the LORD commanded Moses — given through the mediator; the for the children of Israel — to priests and people alike; the in mount Sinai — sealed with its place and authority.

The witnesses agree the verse seals the whole book of Leviticus: all its laws of sacrifice, priesthood, purity, feasts, and vows are gathered up as the commandments of the LORD, given through Moses at Sinai for his people; the book closes by setting its divine authority and its place in the history of redemption, the holy God instructing the holy nation he had redeemed.

So the witnesses make the verse the closing colophon: The witnesses note the these are the commandments: the laws of the book gathered up. The witnesses note the the LORD commanded Moses: given through the mediator. The witnesses note the for the children of Israel: to priests and people alike. The witnesses note the in mount Sinai: sealed with its place and authority. The expositors observe the these are the commandments: the laws of the book gathered up. The expositors observe the the LORD commanded Moses: given through the mediator. The expositors observe the for the children of Israel: to priests and people alike. The expositors observe the in mount Sinai: sealed with its place and authority. The believer is taught to mark the these are the commandments, the the LORD commanded Moses, the for the children of Israel, the in mount Sinai, and to give to God with a willing and reverent heart. The verse thus records that the book closes by gathering all its laws as the commandments of the LORD given at Sinai — the holy God instructing the holy nation he had redeemed.
